probe: Is this type demanded by the DOMAIN, or by the current BEHAVIOR OF A TOOL? If the tool changed tomorrow, would this type still have members?
fired_at: /intent, at the WHAT step — before the trace. A tool-shaped type usually traces upward just fine (that is why the trace does not catch it), so this has to fire earlier and separately.
why:
It catches a type that exists because a tool forced a boundary, not because the domain has one. That failure is invisible to every other gate we run: the type has a real WHAT, a plausible WHY, it traces to a Law, and it accumulates members — so nothing red-flags it. It only becomes visible when the tool changes underneath it and the members stop arriving. By then the type is in the taxonomy, in the MAP, and in everyone's vocabulary.

The second-order reason it matters: tool-shaped types arrive by ACCRETION rather than by decision. Akien, 2026-07-22, on how `session` was born: 'sessions were a thing you kept creating without input from me, so we just started building to the tool and included them as a type.' Nobody ruled that sessions should be a species. CC kept producing them, and the taxonomy grew around the output. A probe fired at /intent is the cheapest place to interrupt that, because it asks the question BEFORE the artifact exists to be rationalized.
yield: UNMEASURED (Law 3). Founding firing count: 0. Its first case is retrospective — see evidence below — which is a hypothesis about what it would have caught, not a measurement of what it did catch. Yield begins accruing at its first live firing.

evidence_retrospective.the_case_that_produced_it:
`session` as a species. Akien, 2026-07-22: 'it used to be that a session was what happened at every compact boundary. And we'd go all the way back to context loaded. You'd have to read the slate to get oriented again. A month later, compact changed, and doesn't wipe you out between sessions. Compact does reduce the context, but you appear to just be able to pick up cleanly now without additional support. And it appears you don't need them as a class anymore?'

The type was pinned to a tool boundary — the compact wipe. The tool changed; the boundary vanished; the type had zero members, no gate, and no owner, and was still sitting in the MAP's species list a month later. This probe, fired at its birth, would have asked: if compact stopped wiping context, would `session` still have members? The honest answer was always no.
evidence_retrospective.what_survived_the_retirement: The NOUN survives, the CLASS does not. The immutable journey is real and exists (~/.claude/projects/*/*.jsonl) — we simply do not own it; the harness writes it. A thing that exists in the world but that we neither own nor gate is not a class in Cairn's sense; it is a citable artifact, and a citation is a field on a node, not a species. `slate` is unaffected and still earns its keep — it ran at this session's open and worked; its boundary just moved from compact to process-start.

how_to_answer_it: Name the tool behavior the type depends on, then imagine it gone. If the type still has members, it is domain-shaped. If it does not, it was a boundary the tool imposed and the type is a shadow of it. A useful tell: tool-shaped types are usually born from ARTIFACTS THAT ARE ALREADY BEING PRODUCED rather than from a stated need — the members precede the intention, which is backwards.
provenance: The corpus's first DISCOVERED probe (2026-07-22) — not extracted from a skill file like /intent's five and /sorted's four, but found by dissecting a real failure the same day the store was chartered. Recorded as CANDIDATE with zero firings, because a probe that has never fired has no yield and tenure is earned by yield, not by how good the story of its birth is (T5.1).
